title: 1981-04-16_Pat_38.txt
key_claims: Dick maps his experience perfectly onto the orthodox Christological formula: "Two Natures, One Person." He concludes that "Thomas" was the adventitious human psyche that combined with his own innate psyche.

Overturning his earlier reincarnation theories, he realizes he was never Thomas in a past life. Instead, Thomas (a literal 1st-century persecuted Christian) invaded his mind, combining with him to form a singular Christ-entity.

He reasons that this requires a literal, unbroken line of apostolic succession—a sequence of true Christian psyches dying and returning to merge with living hosts, perpetually recreating the Ditheon across history.

The resurrection of Jesus was simply the paradigmatic first instance of this; Jesus returned to his disciples as the Paraclete, launching the chain reaction that continues through history to individuals like Dick.
recurring_concepts: **The Adventitious Psyche**: The resurrected personality (e.g., Thomas) that enters the host to form the divine composite.
**The Antioch School**: Dick notes his theory aligns with the Adoptionist theology often associated with Antioch (where the Holy Spirit descended on the human Jesus).
reading_excerpt: Dick maps his experience perfectly onto the orthodox Christological formula: "Two Natures, One Person." He concludes that "Thomas" was the adventitious human psyche that combined with his own innate psyche.
